title: Solar panel cleaning and maintenance: cost and worth it?
description: What solar panel cleaning costs in the UK, how much output dirty panels really lose, and the maintenance that keeps a system earning for 25 years.
blurb: Most UK panels barely need cleaning. Here is what a professional clean costs and when paying actually makes sense.
lede: Solar panels are sold as fit-and-forget, and for the most part that is true. There are no moving parts, the glass is designed to shed rain, and a decent system will tick along for 25 years with very little attention. But two questions come up in almost every owner's first year: do the panels need cleaning, and what should I budget for upkeep? The cleaning industry has an obvious interest in telling you yes and often. The honest answer is more useful. In most of the UK, rain does the bulk of the work for free, and a paid clean only earns its keep in specific situations you can spot from your own generation figures. This guide puts real numbers on dirt, cleaning and the rest of the maintenance budget so you can decide with a calculator rather than a sales pitch.
short_answer: Professional solar panel cleaning costs roughly £4 to £15 per panel in the UK, which usually means £100 to £150 a visit for a typical house. Rain keeps most pitched-roof systems within about 2 to 3 per cent of their clean output, worth only £15 to £25 a year on a 4kW array, so an annual paid clean rarely pays for itself. It is worth paying when monitoring shows a real drop, or for bird droppings, low-pitch roofs and ground mounts. The bigger maintenance cost to plan for is an inverter replacement, typically £500 to £1,000 at some point after year ten.

sections.h2: The maintenance a solar PV system actually needs
sections.html:
A domestic solar system has remarkably little to go wrong. The panels themselves carry performance warranties of 25 years or more and have no moving parts. The component that does wear out is the inverter, the box that converts the panels' DC output into mains AC, and that is a planned replacement rather than maintenance as such. If you are still weighing up a system, our guide to solar panels and how they work covers the parts in plain English.

The genuinely useful routine is short:

  • Check your generation monthly. Almost every modern inverter has an app or web portal. Compare this year's monthly output with last year's. A gradual decline of a percent or two a year is normal panel ageing; a sudden drop of 10 per cent or more means dirt, shading or a fault.
  • Look at the panels a few times a year. From the ground with binoculars is fine. You are looking for bird droppings, heavy moss or lichen at the panel edges, debris, and anything growing tall enough to cast new shade.
  • Listen and look at the inverter. A red light, an error code or a unit that has gone silent is the most common cause of a system quietly producing nothing for weeks.
  • Keep the paperwork. Your MCS certificate, the DNO notification and the warranty documents matter if you sell the house or claim on a warranty, and your buildings insurer should already know the panels exist.

None of that costs anything. The question of paid work comes down to cleaning and the eventual inverter swap, so let us put numbers on both.

sections.h2: What dirty panels actually cost you
sections.html:
Dirt blocks light, and blocked light is lost generation. The industry calls this soiling loss. In the UK the effect is smaller than the cleaning adverts suggest, because our frequent rain rinses panels mounted at a typical roof pitch of 30 to 40 degrees reasonably well. Studies of UK and northern European systems generally put soiling losses on pitched roofs at around 2 to 5 per cent a year, with the higher figures near trees, busy roads, railways and farmland. Panels at a shallow tilt, under 10 degrees or so, rinse poorly and collect a grime line along the bottom edge, and ground-mounted arrays near agricultural dust can do noticeably worse.

Here is what those percentages mean in money on a typical 4kW system generating 3,700 kWh a year. The value of a lost kilowatt-hour depends on whether you would have used it or exported it, so the table uses a blended rate of half self-used and half exported.

Soiling levelTypical situationOutput lost per yearCost per year
2%Normal pitched roof, regular rain74 kWh£15
5%Near trees, a main road or farmland185 kWh£38
10%Heavy bird droppings or a low-pitch roof370 kWh£76
15%Ground mount near dust, never cleaned555 kWh£114

Assumes a 4kW system generating 3,700 kWh a year, with half the lost output valued at 26.11p per kWh (Ofgem cap, July to September 2026) and half at a 15p SEG export rate, a blended 20.6p per kWh. Checked June 2026.

The pattern is clear. A normally sited pitched-roof system loses £15 to £25 a year to dirt, which a £100 to £150 annual clean cannot repay. The economics only flip when something specific is going on: droppings under a gull or pigeon perch, a shallow tilt, lichen taking hold at the edges, or a dusty rural site. Bird droppings deserve particular attention because a single solid deposit can shade one cell hard, and depending on the panel's electronics that can drag down a whole string rather than just the dirty spot.

One more nuance: a kilowatt-hour you lose at midday in summer is often one you would have exported at 5 to 15p rather than used at 26.11p, especially without storage. If you have a battery soaking up your surplus, lost generation costs you closer to the full import rate, which strengthens the case for keeping panels clean. Our solar battery storage guide explains how that shifts the value of every panel kilowatt-hour.

sections.h2: Professional cleaning: what you pay and what you should expect
sections.html:
Professional solar panel cleaning in the UK typically costs £4 to £15 per panel, and most firms quote per visit: roughly £60 to £100 for a small single-storey array, £100 to £150 for a typical two-storey house with 10 to 14 panels, and £150 to £200 where access is awkward or the array is large. Ground-mounted panels are cheaper because nobody needs to work at height.

A proper solar cleaning firm works with deionised (pure) water fed through a soft brush on a telescopic pole, usually from the ground or a ladder rather than from the roof itself. Pure water dries without mineral spotting, so no detergent is needed. That matters for more than appearance:

  • Detergents and hard tap water can void panel warranties. Most manufacturers specify water-only cleaning and prohibit abrasives and pressure washing. Ask any cleaner to confirm their method complies.
  • Pressure washers are out. They can force water past seals, crack glass and damage the anti-reflective coating.
  • Nobody should walk on the panels, and weight on the frames can microcrack cells invisibly.

Before booking, ask three things: do they carry public liability insurance, do they use pure water only, and will they note any visible damage or loose fixings while they are up there. That last point is a genuine bonus of a professional visit, since a cleaner with eyes on the array will often spot lifted cables, slipped tiles or early bird nesting before they become expensive.

sections.h2: DIY cleaning: when it is sensible and when it is not
sections.html:
If your panels are on a ground mount, a flat garage roof you can reach safely, or a bungalow where a pole reaches from the ground, DIY cleaning is straightforward and close to free. Use a soft brush or microfibre head on a telescopic pole, plain water (a hose is fine, though deionised water avoids drying marks in hard-water areas), and no soap. Clean early in the morning or on an overcast day, partly because cold water on hot glass risks thermal shock and partly because cool panels do not flash-dry into streaks.

For a two-storey roof, the calculation changes completely. Working at height kills people every year, and the £100 you save is not worth a fall. Leaning a ladder against guttering to scrub at arm's length is exactly the scenario ladder safety campaigns are written about. If a pole from the ground will not reach, pay a professional or accept the 2 per cent loss; both are rational, climbing is not.

Whatever you do, never scrape at droppings or lichen with anything metal, never use a pressure washer, and never use household glass cleaner or washing-up liquid. The grime that matters comes off with water and a soft brush. Anything that needs more than that probably needs a professional assessment rather than more force.

sections.h2: The rest of the maintenance budget over 25 years
sections.html:
Cleaning is the recurring question, but the bigger lines in a 25-year ownership budget are one-off items. Plan for these and a solar system holds very few surprises:

  • Inverter replacement: £500 to £1,000 fitted, typically once, somewhere between year 10 and year 15. String inverters usually carry 5 to 12 year warranties. Microinverters and power optimisers tend to have 20 to 25 year warranties, which is part of how they justify their higher up-front cost.
  • Bird-proofing: £300 to £600 if pigeons nest under the array. Mesh fitted around the panel edges solves it. Worth doing at the first sign of nesting, because droppings, noise and pecked cabling get worse every season.
  • An electrical health check: £100 to £200 every five years or so is reasonable, checking connections, isolators and inverter operation. There is no legal requirement for routine servicing of a domestic system, so treat anyone selling a mandatory annual service plan with scepticism.
  • Monitoring: free. The inverter app costs nothing and is the single most effective maintenance tool you have, because it catches the expensive failure mode, a system silently producing nothing.

Spread over 25 years, realistic upkeep comes to roughly £40 to £80 a year on average, dominated by that one inverter swap. That figure is already baked into sensible payback calculations, and our guide on whether solar is worth it shows how it fits into the overall sums. If you are trying to squeeze more value from existing panels before spending anything on upkeep, a solar diverter that sends surplus into your hot water cylinder often beats a cleaning contract pound for pound.

Solar panel maintenance is cheap and mostly free. Watch your generation figures monthly, glance at the panels a few times a year, and budget £500 to £1,000 for an inverter replacement after year ten. Cleaning is the part the industry oversells: on a normally pitched UK roof, rain holds soiling losses to around 2 to 3 per cent, worth £15 to £25 a year, so a £100 to £150 annual clean loses you money. Pay for cleaning when there is a reason: bird droppings, a shallow tilt, lichen, a dusty site, or a drop in the monitoring data that dirt would explain. A clean every two to three years, or simply whenever the numbers sag, keeps almost every system within a percent or two of its best for a few pounds a month averaged out. Owners with a battery have slightly more to gain from clean panels, since every lost kilowatt-hour costs them the full import rate rather than the export rate. Either way, let the data decide, not the leaflet through the door.

faq.q: How much does solar panel cleaning cost in the UK?
faq.a: Typically £4 to £15 per panel, which works out at £100 to £150 per visit for a normal two-storey house with 10 to 14 panels. Small single-storey arrays can be £60 to £100, and ground-mounted panels cost less because there is no work at height.

faq.q: Do solar panels in the UK really need cleaning?
faq.a: Usually not on a schedule. UK rain keeps panels at a normal roof pitch within about 2 to 3 per cent of their clean output, worth only £15 to £25 a year on a 4kW system. Cleaning earns its keep where there are bird droppings, a shallow tilt, lichen, nearby trees or farmland dust, or when your monitoring shows an unexplained drop.

faq.q: Can I clean solar panels myself with a hose?
faq.a: Yes, if you can do it safely from the ground, using plain water and a soft brush on a telescopic pole. Never use a pressure washer, detergent or anything abrasive, as these can damage coatings and void the panel warranty, and never climb onto a roof to clean panels.

faq.q: What maintenance do solar panels need each year?
faq.a: Very little. Check generation in the inverter app monthly, look the panels over from the ground a few times a year, and watch for inverter error lights. There is no legal requirement for an annual service. The main planned cost is an inverter replacement of £500 to £1,000 sometime after year ten.

faq.q: Why has my solar generation suddenly dropped?
faq.a: Compare against the same month last year first, since weather varies a lot. A sudden fall of 10 per cent or more usually means an inverter fault or error state, heavy soiling such as bird droppings, or new shading from grown trees or a neighbour's extension. The inverter is the most common culprit and the first thing to check.
